title: A preregistered measurement audit reveals construct-validity limits of alignment-based translation concentration
description: Corpus-derived quantities are increasingly used to measure psychological and cognitive constructs, yet whether such a measure captures its intended construct is seldom validated before it is used for inference. This study treats one such measure as a case study in preregistered construct-validity auditing: translation concentration, a corpus index of how concentrated versus dispersed a word’s translations are in aligned bitext, here used to probe the cross-linguistic grounding of emotion concepts. A preregistered, circularity-avoiding design (English–Japanese) related translation concentration to the bodily grounding of emotion-related words, with grounding indices computed from independent monolingual norms and corpora, and specified a token-level alignment-quality audit as a gate on the predictor, downgrading affected analyses to exploratory if the gate was not met. The audit failed in both arms: alignments were judged correct for only 51.0% (English) and 51.5% (Japanese) of sampled instances and incorrect for 35.3% and 39.0%, and the English emotion-word set was used in non-emotional senses in 70.3% of instances versus 3.0% in Japanese. Critically, misalignment was correlated with the predictor (English low- versus high-concentration words: .427 vs .280; Fisher p = .0110), so low concentration partly reflected poor alignment. The preregistered associations, reported as exploratory, were small and null or inconclusive. A preregistered measurement audit can thus expose a construct-validity failure in a plausible measure before it informs theory: subtitle-alignment-based translation concentration, as operationalized here, did not validly support inference about emotion-word grounding.

title: How Does the Mind Grow? Cross-Cultural Intuitive Theories of Mental Development
description: How does the mind grow? Despite centuries of philosophical and psychological inquiry, little is known about how ordinary people intuitively conceptualize mental development. Across six countries (Aus- tralia, Japan, Mexico, South Africa, the United Kingdom, and the United States), adult participants re- ported their intuitions about mental development by indicating when they think various mental capaci- ties first emerge. Across tasks and cultures, intuitions about mental development were consistently orga- nized along two dimensions: an earlier-developing perceptual and experiential dimension (e.g., seeing, fear, hunger, pain) and a later-developing reflective and evaluative dimension (e.g., reasoning, holding beliefs, self-restraint, pride). Competing models were ruled out, showing that this structure is unique to lay beliefs about mental development. These dimensions also aligned with participants’ intuitions about the origins of mental capacities within a nature–nurture framework. Together, the findings reveal a consistent cross-cultural pattern for reasoning about mental development and illuminate the intuitive architecture of mind perception.

title: Father–child and Mother–child Home Math Activities and Toddlers’ Math Skills: The Role of Children’s Interest in Math
description: Research on mathematical development in early childhood, particularly during the toddler years (typically 18–36 months), remains limited. This study explores the associations between the home math environment (HME), parent-reported toddlers’ interest in math-related activities, and their early math skills. The sample included 420 Finnish toddlers whose early math skills were assessed at an average age of 2 years and 9 months. Both parents (N mothers = 318, N fathers = 221) responded to questionnaires covering aspects of the HME (formal and informal activities) and children’s interest in math activities involving numbers and operations. Parents’ education level, child age, and gender were controlled in the structural equation model, which regressed early math skills on HME, child interest, and controls, as well as child interest on HME and controls. The model explained 43% of the variance in early math skills and 49% and 53% of the variance in the interest variables: Fathers’ responses regarding less formal and more informal HME showed a direct association with children’s early math skills. Furthermore, a greater interest in activities involving numbers, older age, and being a girl were associated with better early math skills. More formal activities were positively associated with children’s interest in number activities, and the indirect effect of formal HME on children’s early math skills was significant and positive. These results suggest strong links between the HME, children’s interest in math activities, and their math skills at toddler age.

title: Reconceptualizing the Internal/External Frame of Reference Model: Achievement-Level–Contrast and the Architecture of Academic Self-Concept
description:
For four decades, the internal/external frame-of-reference (I/E) model has explained a paradox in academic self-concept research: mathematics and verbal achievements are strongly correlated, whereas their self-concepts are weakly related. In classic I/E representations, achievement in one domain positively predicts same-domain self-concept but can negatively predict contrasting-domain self-concept. I offer a conceptual-methodological reconceptualization of the I/E model, arguing that the classic representation captures this pattern but leaves the achievement-information architecture implicit in adjusted paths among correlated predictors. This framing advances theory by showing how statistical parameterizations shape conclusions about comparison processes.
Using analytic demonstrations and a PISA2000 benchmark reanalysis, I evaluate the classic I/E representation, Difference I/E representation, Achievement-Level–Contrast I/E representation (ALC-I/E), and a Full Level–Contrast extension. Difference I/E foregrounds relative achievement standing, but its manifest-score, outcome-specific operationalization does not provide an integrated decomposition of the achievement space. ALC-I/E provides the central reconceptualization, shifting emphasis from path signs to information structure. Through an orthogonal sum-and-difference transformation, it re-expresses mathematics and verbal achievement as achievement level and achievement contrast, separating academic standing from relative mathematics–verbal standing. This preserves the empirical core of the I/E model while clarifying its informational architecture rather than treating negative cross-domain paths as self-explanatory mechanisms. Achievement level positively predicted both self-concepts, whereas achievement contrast more strongly differentiated them. In the Full Level–Contrast extension, achievement level mapped primarily onto self-concept level, whereas achievement contrast mapped primarily onto self-concept contrast

title: Meditative Development and Psychological Adverse Effects, Challenging, Unpleasant, and Negative Experiences: A Phenomenological Study of Advanced Meditators
description:
Objective: To investigate meditation-related psychological adverse effects, challenging, unpleasant, and negative experiences (MRPAEs) across meditative development and specifically advanced meditation, an emerging domain in contemplative science that remains understudied despite its potential for radically positively transforming, and in some cases destabilizing, mental health.
Design: A qualitative study using semi-structured phenomenological interviews and inductive, content-driven thematic analysis. Theoretical saturation was monitored iteratively, interrater reliability was assessed throughout the coding process, and methodological reporting adhered to the Consolidated Criteria for Reporting Qualitative Research (COREQ).
Participants: 28 advanced meditators with extensive long-term practice histories who were proficient in advanced practices.
Setting: Remote, in-depth interviews conducted with advanced meditators with diverse contemplative backgrounds.
Results: Across 55 interviews, participants described wide-ranging negative and appraisal-dependent experiences spanning affective, cognitive, relational, clinical, and existential domains. Seven major themes were identified: (1) broadly negative cognitive, attentional, and existential disruptions; (2) negative affect, including mood disturbance, anxiety, and fear; (3) adverse impacts on life trajectory and interpersonal relationships; (4) negative clinical and non-clinical transformative experiences, including clinically significant psychiatric symptoms and trauma activation; (5) foundational instability associated with specific meditative insights; (6) ambiguous experiences of context-dependent valence; and (7) resolution strategies, including meditative techniques, teacher guidance, and interpersonal support.
Conclusions: Advanced meditation can precipitate significant psychological challenges across multiple domains. Although meditative development is also associated with profound positive transformation, the

title: Putting the Cart Before the Horse: Trait Disgust Propensity Cannot Mediate Immediate Stimulus Responses
description: This work critiques a recent neuroimaging study to demonstrate why disgust propensity and disgust sensitivity cannot function as mediators of immediate stimulus responses. By proposing a conceptually valid framework, it highlights that proper model specification is essential for meaningful mediation analyses in disgust research.

title: Facilitation by Irrelevant Distractors Under High Perceptual Load: Suppression or Controlled Distractor Prioritization?
description: Perceptual load theory is a key framework in visual attention research, demonstrating that under high-load conditions, fully irrelevant distractors are no longer processed and do not disrupt task performance. In our recent work (Manini et al., 2026), we replicated this effect. However, we also found that under high load, the distractor not only failed to impair performance but actually led to faster reaction times (RTs) in distractor-present trials. In the attentional capture literature, this reversed effect is typically considered to reflect distractor suppression. However, in the perceptual load task, where the distractor is placed outside the relevant search array, suppression should not logically lead to faster responses. Furthermore, the faster responses were accompanied by increased error rates, leading us to hypothesize that this reversed effect may reflect some form of distractor prioritization rather than distractor suppression. To test this hypothesis, we first analyzed the consistency of this effect across all available open-data resources using the Forster and Lavie (2008b) task. We then reanalyzed the same data, incorporating the spatial proximity between target and distractor as an additional variable: distractors appearing near the target strongly drove the reversed RT effect, while distractors presented far from the target led to increased error rates. These findings challenge the idea that high perceptual load prevents the processing of irrelevant stimuli. Moreover, they suggest that faster RTs in distractor-present conditions do not necessarily indicate distractor suppression, as commonly assumed in attentional capture research, offering new insight into the interpretation of behavioral observations in this field.

title: Contralateral thermal pain and naturally occurring muscle pain have different effects on force production during a fixed perceived effort handgrip task
description:
Background: In the presence of pain, performance during imposed workload motor tasks may be maintained at the cost of a higher perceived effort. This study investigated how experimentally induced contralateral thermal pain and naturally occurring muscle pain influence force production during a fixed perceived effort task. 
Methods: Forty young adults performed intermittent isometric handgrip contractions at light (13/100) or hard (50/100) perceived effort intensities across two visits. Each visit included an experimental condition, featuring contralateral thermal pain in block 1, and a control condition, with non-painful warm stimulation in block 2. Participants completed 10 blocks (five repetitions of block 1, block 2), with force production and electromyography continuously recorded. Participants rated thermal and muscle pain after each block. 
Results: Force production was higher during thermal pain compared to control, with no interaction with perceived effort intensity. Regression analysis showed that each one-unit increase in muscle pain (0–100 scale) corresponded to a reduction in peak force (0.45 N), mean force (0.32 N), and force integral (1.02 N·s). 
Conclusions: During a fixed perceived effort task, contralateral thermal pain increases force production. In contrast, muscle pain is associated with reduced force production. This study suggests that the type of pain has a varied effect on force production during voluntary motor tasks. This study questions the well-accepted inhibitory effect of pain on force production and encourages future research to use fixed perceived effort tasks to fully appraise the effects of different pain types on force production.

title: Towards a Behavioural Systems Map of the University System in Relation to Student Physical Activity and Sedentary Behaviour: An Exploratory Study
description: University students often exhibit high levels of sedentary behaviour (SB) and low levels of physical activity (PA). Previous efforts to increase students’ movement have focused on leisure-time PA or overlooked the university environment and the education system. Embedding PA into university education offers one potentially fruitful avenue for increasing student movement, yet understanding is needed of how and why the university education system influences students’ movement before effective interventions can be developed. This study used a novel behavioural systems mapping method to explore the perceived behaviours, actors, and influences affecting students’ PA and SB during university education. The aim of this exploratory study was to develop a preliminary behavioural systems map based on a combined dataset of interviews with 20 university students and 38 staff in a variety of roles. Data were qualitatively analysed to construct a map of the perceived actors, behaviours, and influences affecting students’ movement behaviours, as well as perceived causal links between them. Influences were further analysed using the capability, opportunity, and motivation model of behaviour (COM-B). The resulting map would need further data input and corroboration to be considered a comprehensive map, yet the results presented in this study provide a useful starting point in this regard. The final map had over 100 elements and connections in total, with 31 different behaviours performed by various actors identified, demonstrating the system complexity as described by participants. Three clusters of behaviours and influences were identified: student movement in classes, student movement in private study time, and wider system influences and behaviours. Across all clusters, a key behaviour perceived as influential by participants was policy makers prioritising student movement in decision making, suggesting policy-level change is essential for long-term impact. Many identified barrie

title: Perceptions, attitudes, and concerns of underrepresented groups regarding the use of big data in children and young people’s healthcare research – a systematic literature review
description:
Children and young people (CYP), and those from underserved groups, remain underrepresented in biomedical research. Understanding their perspectives on large-scale, data-driven healthcare research is essential to ensuring that such approaches are acceptable, ethically conducted, and equitable. This review is the first to systematically examine the perceptions, attitudes, and concerns of underrepresented groups regarding the use of big data in CYP healthcare research.  

Seven bibliographic databases, ASSIA, CINAHL, EMBASE, MEDLINE, PsycINFO, Scopus, and Web of Science, were searched alongside grey literature sources. A mixed-methods systematic review design was used. Included studies captured the views of CYP, particularly those from underserved groups, as well as their parents, carers, or guardians, regarding the use of big data in paediatric healthcare research. Study quality was appraised using the Mixed Methods Appraisal Tool (MMAT), and findings were integrated through narrative synthesis. 

Seventeen studies were included, capturing perspectives from 6261 CYP, parents, and carers. Underrepresented groups expressed broadly favourable, but conditional, support for the use of big data in paediatric healthcare research. Willingness to share data was shaped by participant characteristics, lived experience, and trust in institutions. Perceived benefits included societal and public health gains, the opportunity to empower others with similar experiences, and the advancement of scientific progress. Concerns centred on privacy, the risk of re-identification, data misuse, commercial exploitation, and limited transparency in data governance. Conditions for acceptable data use included robust anonymisation, independent ethical oversight, and developmentally appropriate consent processes. 

Underrepresented groups hold broadly supportive, but conditional, views on the use of big data in paediatric healthcare research. Embedding inclusive governance practices and engaging t

title: A cross-generational investigation of physical health, mental health, and lifestyle during the COVID-19 pandemic in The Netherlands: results from network analyses
description: The coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID 19) pandemic substantially affected daily life and health. This preregistered study used nationally representative data from three Dutch Corona Public Health Monitors to conceptualise health during the pandemic as a system of mutually reinforcing components across four generations (166,252 youth, 69,750 young adults, 185,443 adults, and 171,076 elderly individuals). First, per generation, sample characteristics were compared between low- and high-pandemic-impact groups, using chi-square tests (Aim 1). Second, cross-sectional network analyses (Mixed Graphical Models) were used to examine how physical health, mental health, and lifestyle were interrelated within all age and impact groups (Aim 2). Finally, all networks were compared to distinguish shared and age-/impact-specific constellations of key health factors (Aim 3). Across generations, experiencing financial difficulties was identified as a key factor increasing the likelihood of belonging to the high-impact group (Aim 1). In all networks (Aim 2), perceived general health emerged as one of the most central nodes, with strong connections to mental health and physical activity. In addition, unique key health factors were identified in all networks, highlighting the distinctiveness of each network per generation and pandemic-impact group (Aim 3). Together, these analyses generate actionable insights for national, regional, and local policymakers by providing a nuanced understanding of how pandemic-related stress is embedded in health networks across the life course. This can inform the targeted allocation of resources and the development of age-specific strategies for future public health crises, allowing strategies and interventions to be tailored to the needs of specific generations and those most affected.

title: Investigating musical pitch as a cue for resolving prosodic stress ambiguity in artificial speech: Evidence from Dutch and Chinese listeners
description: Over the last few decades, comparative research has explored whether language and music rely on shared or distinct cognitive and neural mechanisms. Previous research shows robust correlations between music pitch and linguistic prosody (e.g., Jansen et al., 2023), yet neuroscientific evidence does not confirm neural overlap (Chen et al., 2018; 2023), making conclusions about their underlying organization difficult. Predictive Coding Theory (PCT - Friston, 2003; 2005, 2010; Friston & Kiebel, 2009) has been proposed as an organizing principle of shaping perception in both domains (Fiveash, Tillmann & Sammler, 2024), reflecting the brain’s effort to reduce perceptual uncertainty and impose meaningful structure. To test interactions between linguistic and music pitch, we examined ambiguous contexts where pitch processing operates across domains. Using bisyllabic artificial words with uncertain prosodic stress patterns (e.g., bobo), we tested whether tonal (Chinese) and non-tonal (Dutch) listeners, considering music pitch perception scores, use musical pitch cues aligned with stress patterns (high vs. lower F0) to resolve ambiguity. An online forced-choice task measured syllable preferences and facilitation. Results were mixed. We found potential involvement of L1 prosodic transfer in Dutch (and possibly L2 in Chinese), suggesting listeners may interpret musical pitch through patterns learned in their native (or second) language. At the same time, concurrent musical pitch influenced stress judgments regardless of music perception skills. However, the absence of response time effects and alternative explanations limit interpreting this as evidence for shared linguistic and musical pitch processing under perceptual uncertainty.

title: Eye-head Coordination as a Signature of Bouldering Expertise: an Embodied Cognition Approach
description: Climbing demands that the performer see, balance, and move as a single act, making it a compelling case for embodied accounts of cognition. Yet climbing research has largely measured body morphology, gaze, or movement in isolation, and gaze has typically been recorded separately from the head that carries the eyes. We recorded gaze and head movement simultaneously, using a head-mounted eye tracker with an integrated inertial sensor, in 22 climbers spanning the full grade range from novice to competition athlete, on a naturalistic bouldering route. Principal component analysis revealed that gaze and head movement did not separate into distinct channels but co-varied as a single dimension reflecting the magnitude of visual-postural exploration; this dimension, together with the temporal organization of head movement, predicted climbing grade (R^2=0.615). The anthropometric measures most emphasized in climbing:  arm span, BMI, and ape index, did not predict grade independently of sex, although body morphology helped distinguish climber subgroups. These findings indicate that the eyes and head operate as a single exploration system whose coordination, rather than the body’s structure, marks bouldering expertise, and support an embodied account in which skilled perception and action are inseparable.

title: Public acceptance of climate scientists’ engagement is broad, but not without limits
description: Climate scientists increasingly engage in actions to draw attention to climate change, yet evidence on how engagement shapes public perceptions remains mixed. A survey of 1,348 Americans shows that public support extends well beyond research and teaching, but declines for more norm-challenging, and especially, disruptive actions. Political polarisation was evident across all forms of engagement, but was strongest for contentious, weaker for disruptive, and smallest for mainstream actions.

title: Does sensorimotor adaptation affects detection of and pupil response to motor perturbations?
description: Much recent research on sensorimotor adaptation has focussed on its cognitive side and how adaptation differs when it it cognitively controlled. Here, we investigate more closely what makes different types of perturbations detectable. We used different perturbation schedules to contrast how the magnitude of a motor perturbation and the associated error signal contribute to its detectability. We manipulated perturbation either following an unpredictable de Bruijn sequence, maximising the error signal, or a ramp perturbation, incrementally increasing perturbations and thus minimising error by allowing continuous adaptation. We did this in two different experiments using two perturbed everyday actions, walking with a split-belt speed perturbation (experiment 1) and grasping with a visuo-haptic size mismatch (experiment 2). In each experiment, N=48 participants were asked to judge the relevant dimension, either the relative speed of the treadmill belts, or the relative size of the objects to be grasped, while we measured participants' pupil dilation and investigated if this could serve as a no-report marker of perturbation detection. We found that participants reliably adapted to the incrementally introduced ramp perturbation and concurrently showed slightly reduced perturbation detection relative to de Bruijn sequence perturbations. Tonic pupil-dilation parameters were noisy, but showed differences depending on both perturbation magnitude and the type of perturbation, consistent with an effect of uncertainty. Pupil responses also differed depending on the detection response, but not sufficiently to allow trialwise classification in these data. These patterns occurred both in walking and in grasping, with only minor differences, suggesting that the underlying mechanisms may be similar and potentially generalisable across motor actions.

title: A cross-cultural analysis of religious fasting and well-being using  15 years of internet search data
description: Fasting is historically and cross-culturally widespread and prescribed by many world religions. Yet, its effects on psychological health and well-being remain poorly understood. We analyzed 15 years of internet search data to examine how one religious fast—Ramadan—affects society-level well-being in religiously homogenous countries (i.e., ≥75% Muslim population). We operationalized well-being using composite indices of Google searches indicative of poor psychological well-being and mental health. Our analysis reveals robust evidence that Ramadan predicts improved well-being in Muslim-majority countries. Further, leveraging the year-to-year variation in Ramadan intensity due to seasonal variation in day length, we also found that longer Ramadan fasts—which occur in warmer months and farther from the equator—predict significantly more positive effects. This suggests that benefits of the Ramadan fast may at least partly be attributable to the difficulty of the ritual. We discuss possible mechanisms and implications of these findings for understanding the cultural evolution of ritual practices.

title: Across Minds and Masterpieces:  Identifying "Shared Resonance" with Visual Art via Multi-Brain Hyperscanning
description: A long-standing argument in art studies suggests that certain artistic examples have some manner of universal resonance or lasting appeal that transcends local conditions and connects with many viewers across time, tastes, and context. However, finding ways to actually assess this, not to mention whether this could be detected across artworks and individuals, remains an empirical challenge. In this exploratory study, we introduce a novel approach to this challenge, borrowing an emerging technique from social, multi-participant neuroscience and Network Modelling methods to examine possible relationships between collective appreciation of media and shared inter-brain synchrony, or similar brain activity patterns across an entire viewer cohort. Thirty participants viewed 60 Western and Japanese paintings, previously shown to be relatively more/less popular among the public, individually, for 30 seconds each. Brain activity was recorded using functional near-infrared spectroscopy (fNIRS) over prefrontal, anterior temporal, and right inferior frontal gyrus (rIFG) regions. Especially the rIFG had been recently suggested, in an exploratory paper introducing this method, to suggest potential relations between groupwise synchrony, behavioral ratings, and a data-driven means of identifying "resonance" with music and video advertisements. Essentially replicating the past findings, shared synchrony (Network Density) over the rIFG across our viewer cohort did, indeed, significantly correlate to collective appraisal, notably willingness to pay to see the artworks. The result is discussed in terms of functional role, temporal versus static media, and considering the overall potential of this multi-brain hyperscanning method for opening a new avenue into understanding and empirically capturing our collective appeal of art as it manifests across brains.

title: From Digital Data to Psychological Insights: Making Sense of Mobile-Sensing Data Through Integrative Preprocessing Pipelines
description:
Psychological research has long centered around questionnaire assessments, but
now digital devices, especially smartphones, enable the collection of real-world
behavioral data through mobile sensing. While this data collection method offers
unique opportunities, it also introduces new methodological challenges, as
mobile-sensing data are highly complex and high in dimensionality (i.e., time-stamped
events with millisecond resolution), requiring advanced preprocessing to derive
psychologically meaningful variables. This article highlights these challenges by
reviewing the current state of data preprocessing based on app usage logs from
smartphones. Afterwards, it presents three preprocessing cases that vary in complexity
across the dimensions of data enrichment—which involves adding context to raw data
by integrating information from external and internal sources (including Ecological
Momentary Assessments)—and data aggregation—which entails summarizing data in
different ways, from basic descriptive statistics to sophisticated machine learning
models. For each case, potential pitfalls are identified, and extensions are discussed to
refine our preprocessing pipelines and accommodate different data types and research
questions. By outlining these preprocessing strategies, this manuscript demonstrates
the rich potential of mobile-sensing data for extracting nuanced behavioral variables
beyond simple person-level summaries, and aims to inspire the development of more
advanced research questions based on sensing data.

title: Investigating Everyday Music Choice on Smartphones: The Role of Mood States and Personality Traits
description: Digitalization has created unprecedented freedom of choice in music consumption, highlighting intra- and interindividual differences in everyday listening behavior. To shed light on the factors involved in natural music choices, the present study collected 1,631 music-listening sessions from 110 participants over 14 consecutive days using smartphones for both passive and active ambulatory assessments. We obtained smartphone-sensed music-listening records and experience-sampled mood states, along with Big Five personality traits from traditional surveys. Using multilevel regressions, we predicted momentary music choice in terms of musical valence and energy from concurrent mood states, enduring personality traits, and their interactions. As preregistered, we expected to replicate past findings on mood-congruent music choice and associations between personality and music choice, as well as theory-based interaction effects. However, our models showed that mood and personality accounted for only a small fraction of variance in music choice, with one significant effect indicating that people in more activated mood states chose more energetic songs. Beyond that, our models showed no effects for chosen musical valence and energy. We discuss design-related factors (e.g., operationalization and ecological validity) and outline future avenues for smartphone-based research, with particular emphasis on the role of situational context in everyday music choice.

title: Momentary purpose and cognitive function in everyday life: Evidence from the Purpose and Engagement in Everyday Life study
description:
Objectives: Purpose in life is associated with better cognition in middle and older adulthood. Less is known about how purpose supports cognitive function in moments throughout the day in daily life.
Methods: Participants in the Purpose and Engagement in Everyday Life (PEEL) study (N=2577; Meanage=63.51 [SD=11.24], 60% female, 27% Black) completed an ecological momentary assessment protocol in which they rated their momentary purpose, completed three cognitive tasks, and reported on three cognitive lapses three times a day for eight days. Participants also rated their momentary meaning in life, happiness, and sadness. 
Results: Multilevel modeling of nearly 48,000 assessments indicated that in moments participants felt more purposeful than their own average, they performed faster on momentary measures of attention (b=-.06, SE=.01, p<.001) and executive function (b=-.11, SE=.04, p=.011) but not visual memory. Momentary meaning was associated with attention (b=-.06, SE=.02, p<.001) but not executive function or visual memory. Momentary happiness and sadness were largely unrelated to momentary cognitive performance. Momentary purpose was associated with fewer lapses in concentration (OR=.94, 95% CI=.92, .96, p<.001) and unrelated to memory lapses; the same pattern emerged for meaning, happiness, and sadness (reverse direction). The associations between purpose and momentary cognition were generally similar across age, sex, race, ethnicity, and education.
Discussion: Higher purpose relative to one’s average supports better cognitive performance in daily life. These cognitive benefits do not extend to hedonic well-being. If supported by experimental evidence, purpose in life may be a target of intervention to improve cognitive function in daily life.

title: Limited Information Model Fit Test of Binary Item Response Theory Model with Missing Responses
description: Even though missing data are prevalent in applications of item response theory (IRT), there has been a lack of research on the global model fit of IRT models with missing data. This study proposes two limited information fit statistics under full information maximum likelihood estimation for binary IRT models, related to the M_2 statistics of Maydeu-Olivares and Joe (2005). For a baseline model, we choose a log-linear model up to pairwise interaction effects based on the observations that M_2=0 with df=0 for this model under complete data as the first- and second-order model-implied marginals exactly recover those of sample and that two-parameter logistic IRT models are special cases of the model. We develop a computationally efficient generalized expectation-maximization algorithm (Dempster et al., 1977) to estimate the log-linear model under missing data. The two proposed statistics are motivated by G^2_dif of Fuchs (1982) and D_m of Rubin (1987) in multiple imputation, referred to as G^2_dif and \tilde{M}_2. Due to exponentially increasing cells (i.e., two to the power of the number of items), we discuss poor man's data augmentation of Wei and Tanner (1990) for \tilde{M}_2. We show that the two proposed statistics perform well throughout a simulation study and an application to PISA mathematics achievement data, among which \tilde{M}_2 shows the most promising result. We complete the study by discussing several important topics for the global model fit of IRT models under missing data.

title: Composite Marginal Likelihood Estimation of High-Dimensional Confirmatory Item Response Theory Model
description: In item response theory (IRT), the two-tier IRT model can be considered a general confirmatory IRT model, in which multiple correlated primary factors and orthogonal specific factors are loaded onto items. Although full information maximum likelihood estimation combined with the dimension reduction technique has been successful, it becomes computationally infeasible when the number of primary factors increases. In this study, we propose a composite marginal likelihood (CML) estimation via the expectation-maximization algorithm to alleviate this computational demand. We adopt the pairwise fitting approach, where item responses are divided into subsets according to primary factor pairs. Combined with the dimension reduction technique, this approach can, in certain cases, reduce the required numerical integration to as few as three latent variables, regardless of the total number of primary and specific factors in the model. The potential of the CML method for the two-tier IRT model is demonstrated by a simulation study and applications to two empirical datasets.

title: Beyond treatment: Parents’ Experiences of Insurance, Legal, and Financial Hardships During Childhood Cancer Survivorship
description:
Background and aims: Childhood cancer survival rates have improved substantially, leading to a growing population of survivors and increasing attention to long-term survivorship. Beyond the long-term medical and psychosocial impact on affected children, their parents may experience persistent financial, insurance-related, and legal hardships. The dynamic interplay between these challenges has not yet been comprehensively examined. This study aimed to explore parents’ lived experiences with these challenges from a holistic perspective.
Methods: A qualitative cross-sectional study using focus groups was conducted in Switzerland with adult survivors of childhood and adolescent cancer and with parents of survivors. In total, 11 focus groups with 18 parents and 11 survivors were conducted. Data were collected using a semi-structured interview guide and analyzed using qualitative content analysis with a combined deductive and inductive approach.
Results: Eight categories of long-term hardships were identified. Parents reported persistent financial strain due to treatment-related costs, ongoing medical expenses, and income loss. Insurance-related challenges included high supplementary premiums, co-payments, and uncertainty regarding coverage responsibilities between health and disability insurance. Legal challenges were less frequently discussed but included uncertainty regarding rights and the need for ongoing advocacy. Overall, the findings highlight the interdependence of financial, insurance-related, and legal hardships.
Conclusion: Childhood cancer survivorship is associated with persistent and interconnected socioeconomic challenges for families. More integrated support systems and improved coordination between services are needed to address long-term hardships.

title: EVALUATING THE BIOLOGICAL RELEVANCE OF THE RELATIVISTIC THEORY OF CONSCIOUSNESS
description: According to the Relativistic Theory of Consciousness by Lahav and Neemeh, consciousness is not an absolute feature but a relativistic one that depends on the reference system of the observer. The theory offers a new, thought-provoking view on consciousness, but also raises critical issues. First: What is the fundamental precondition of consciousness which the Relativistic Theory may be based upon? Although this aspect does not question the relativistic principle as such, it still remains open. Therefore, a Comparator Model of consciousness will be discussed in relation to the Relativistic Theory of Consciousness. Second: What is the Relativistic Theory’s biological relevance? This solely depends on the theory’s real-world applicability because this is it what the authors fundamentally envisage by their statement that we just need to move to the appropriate frame of reference in order to measure phenomenal properties. Although the mathematical formulation of changing from a third-person to a first-person frame of reference is possible when based on suitable premises, and may well be internally consistent, this does not per se prove any corresponding biological reality. Thus, the technological requirements for such a transition are a most critical issue. The obstacles in real-life will be addressed.

title: Power output feedback improves cycling performance without affecting the perception of voluntariness in task failure
description: The perception of voluntariness in task failure during extreme physical effort is debated in endurance sports. This study tested whether power meter data availability affects perceived voluntariness and performance in cyclists accustomed to power meters. Forty trained cyclists completed two counterbalanced maximal incremental tests: one without feedback and one with real-time power visualization. Confirmatory analyses showed that power visualization increased maximal aerobic power (MAP) without affecting VO2MAX, maximal heart rate, RPE, or perceived voluntariness. Exploratory analyses revealed an order effect: participants who first performed the test with power data perceived their decision to stop as more voluntary in that condition compared to the subsequent no-feedback test. This effect was absent in the reverse order group. No order effects were found for other variables. Cyclists’ performance judgments aligned with actual MAP differences. These findings show that access to power data enhances MAP. Although the main analysis pointed to a null effect on perceived voluntariness of the decision to stop, an exploratory order effect suggested that removing power data after prior exposure reduces perceived control over task failure decisions, warranting further investigation. This study highlights the role of performance feedback in shaping objective and subjective performance in cycling.

title: Myths About Sexual Violence in Online versus Offline Settings: Unveiling the Impact of the Cyberculture
description: Beliefs about sexual violence influence judgments and perpetration of violence against women. While offline sexual and intimate partner violence myths are well-studied, the rise of cyber-sexual violence raises questions about the specificity of related beliefs. Across two studies with Spanish- and English-speaking participants (N = 979), we examined the distinction and overlap among cyber-sexual violence myths, offline sexual violence myths, and offline intimate partner violence myths, and their unique role in predicting online victim blaming. Using confirmatory factor analyses, we compared different models—including unidimensional, correlated-factor, and bifactor-(S-1)—including later victim blaming as a criterion variable. Results indicated that cyber-sexual violence myths, offline sexual violence myths, and offline intimate partner violence myths are empirically distinct yet positively correlated, with moderate shared variance, especially between offline myths, reflecting a common ideological basis rooted in sexism. Crucially, cyber-sexual violence myths most strongly predicted online victim blaming, highlighting the need for interventions.

title: Correlated motion information improves subsecond visual time perception
description: Continuous visual information, like a conductor’s baton, is used to execute temporal behaviors like playing music with others. And yet, research in time perception has yielded the finding that time perception is superior in audition compared to vision. This finding depends on discrete stimuli, particularly visual flashes of light, which do not match how most temporal events unfold in the real world. We hypothesized that sub-second visual time perception would be improved by a moving stimulus relative to discrete flashes. We found that a continuous visual stimulus that resembled a bouncing ball dramatically improved time-interval discrimination performance over flashes. In subsequent experiments, we showed that the continuous visual stimulus improved performance over the discrete flashes because bounces are better events and because it had motion. When the correlation between the motion of the stimulus and the time intervals it depicted was intact, the motion provided extra temporal information that participants used to produce good performance. These results provide evidence that the asymmetry seen in the literature that time perception in audition is superior to vision is not universal. Continuous information from a moving stimulus can be exploited to produce good sub-second visual time perception.

title: Sociocultural Cannabis Attitudes in Relation to Personality and Cannabis Use: A Bayesian Network Approach
description:
Objective: Risky cannabis use is shaped by a myriad of factors, including individual and environmental factors. Individual level factors such as personality traits have well documented links to increased risky cannabis use, but how they interact with cannabis context to influence cannabis use has not yet been examined. This study examined the influences of personality traits, cannabis attitudes, and cultural context on symptoms related to cannabis use disorder. Understanding this link is important because it can help explain why cannabis use patterns vary across people and settings in service of more precise prevention and intervention strategies. 
Method: Fifty-eight adults from the U.S. and 81 adults from the Netherlands completed the Cannabis Use Disorder Identification Test (CUDIT), NEO Five Factor Inventory (NEO-FFI), and Cannabis Culture Questionnaire (CCQ). Bayesian network analyses were conducted to estimate conditional dependencies and probabilistic relationships among risky cannabis use, personality traits, and cannabis attitudes. 
Results: Personal and perceived peer positive cannabis attitudes formed a tightly interconnected cluster. Personality traits exhibited selective interconnections, with associations primarily occurring among personality domains rather than with cannabis attitudes or use severity. CUDIT scores had a direct negative association with conscientiousness. Legal context was the most highly connected node, linking cannabis use severity, positive cannabis attitudes, and multiple personality traits. 
Conclusions: These findings indicate that cannabis use severity is embedded within a broader network of individual factors that include personality and cannabis attitudes that may be shaped by contextual influences and provide support for culturally sensitive prevention and intervention efforts.

title: Spectral change or Jensen gap? Log-ratio baseline correction for time-frequency M/EEG is negatively biased
description:
Background: Time-frequency M/EEG analysis generally involves baseline correction to measure spectral changes following events of interest. The decibel or ``log-ratio'' baseline correction method divides spectral power in a target time window by average baseline power and log-transforms this ratio. This approach has several desirable properties and is ubiquitously available in popular software tools. However, we show that due to the concavity of the logarithm function, it follows from Jensen's inequality that log-ratio baseline correction is negatively biased, falsely indicating a decrease in spectral power when none exists.
New Method: We propose an unbiased alternative to log-ratio correction in which mean log-transformed baseline power is subtracted from log-transformed power in a target time window. This ``mean-log-ratio'' approach preserves the advantages of log-ratio correction without bias is easily implemented.
Results: Using real and simulated data, we find that log-ratio baseline correction can cause true increases in spectral power to be incorrectly measured as decreases. This downward bias is evident across software implementations of this correction method.
Comparison with existing methods: Whereas downward bias was evident for log-ratio baseline correction, simple mean-subtraction correction (in which mean baseline power is subtracted) was not biased. Similarly, our proposed mean-log-ratio method faithfully reflected true increases in spectral power.
Conclusions: We recommend against log-ratio baseline correction due to its negative bias. Even when analyses primarily examine differences between conditions rather than absolute spectral change from baseline, a biased baseline correction method can influence how results are interpreted. For researchers compelled by the advantages of log-ratio correction, we recommend mean-log-ratio correction.

title: Visual preferences across species: a comparative framework
description: Human preferences for visual features such as curvature, visual structure, biological motion, and color have rarely been studied from a comparative perspective, and are often interpreted as reflecting distinctively human aesthetic processes. We review evidence from primates, birds, fish, and insects showing that comparable preferences are widespread across animal species. These preferences often emerge early, sometimes without prior exposure, but remain highly flexible: they are shaped by developmental trajectories, experience, behavioral goals, multisensory input, physiological state, and ecological context. Mechanistic evidence suggests that these preferences emerge from interactions between species-specific sensory tuning and higher-order neural systems involved in attention, valuation, and action. These preferences help orient behavior toward ecologically relevant features of the environment, supporting foraging, oviposition, social attachment, and social learning. Together, these findings challenge the view that human visual preferences reflect uniquely human aesthetic processes. Instead, they suggest that such preferences are better understood as flexible outputs of perceptual and motivational systems that are widely distributed across species and have been reshaped by human evolution, development, and culture.

title: Parallel Claustral Networks for Behavioral Control in Health and Disease
description:
The claustrum is among the most highly interconnected structures in the mammalian brain, with reciprocal projections spanning the cortical mantle and a growing roster of functions that range from sleep regulation to attentional control. 

Here we review current understanding of claustral circuit organization and function, synthesizing findings from anatomical, physiological, computational, and behavioral studies. We argue that a unifying logic underlies the apparent diversity of claustral functions: the claustrum operates through projection-defined subnetworks whose input selectivity, output targets, and local circuit interactions collectively determine their functional contribution, and whose impact on cortical targets varies with arousal and ongoing brain state. 

Corticoclaustral connectivity is organized into matched input-output modules, local inhibitory circuits confer spatial filtering properties that amplify strong cortical signals while suppressing weaker ones, and claustral output populations differ in their laminar targets, postsynaptic cell-type preferences, and molecular properties, producing diverse and context-dependent effects on cortical dynamics. 

These circuit properties translate into claustral regulation of cortical population dynamics across multiple timescales, from the moment-to-moment coordination of brain states to the consolidation of frontal control strategies. In pain and addiction, specific claustral projection populations are selectively dysregulated, and distinct subnetworks exert opposing influences on nociceptive sensitivity and drug-related behavior according to their cortical targets. 

Together, these findings position the claustrum as a modular subcortical hub for the selective regulation of distributed cortical networks and identify it as a candidate therapeutic target for disorders characterized by dysregulated cortical network activity, including chronic pain, addiction, and impulsivity.

title: Ingressing Minds: Causal Patterns Beyond Genetics and Environment in Natural, Synthetic, and Hybrid Embodiments
description: I argue that the emerging sciences of synthetic morphology and diverse intelligence suggest non-physicalist models of mind and show how they can be empirically investigated. Whence the anatomical, physiological, molecular-biological, and behavioral properties of engineered new beings that have never before existed, and do not have a history of selection? Understanding, predicting, and guiding new forms of life and mind requires characterizing a structured latent space of patterns. Developmental, synthetic, and behavioral biology should take seriously, and exploit, the kinds of non-physicalist ideas that are already a staple of Platonist mathematics. I propose the following hypotheses. (1) Patterns in this space span a highly variable degree of agency, comprising a spectrum ranging from static truths studied by mathematicians to active ones studied by behavioral scientists (i.e., some patterns on the same spectrum as mathematical truths are kinds of minds). (2) The relationship between mind and body is the same as the relationship between causally instructive mathematical facts and physics. (3) Living beings have no monopoly on the “free lunches” provided by the ingression of these patterns into the physical world. While traditional computationalist views of living and cognitive systems are insufficient, my framework erases artificial distinctions between organisms and machines, framing all physical constructs (natural or engineered) as being, to various degrees, in-formed by patterns from the latent space. I sketch a research program, already begun, inspired by these ideas. Such frameworks, while contradicting long-held assumptions of both mechanists and organicists, could have many implications for evolutionary biology, regenerative medicine, AI, and the ethics of synthbiosis with the forthcoming immense diversity of morally important beings.

title: Serial dependence is unchanged by confirmatory but reversed by error feedback
description: Perceptual decisions are shaped not only by current sensory input but also by recent experience. Serial dependence exposes an internal brain process that biases current decisions toward previous ones, even in the absence of feedback about their correctness. When available, feedback on one’s choices is an external factor that also shapes subsequent decisions. However, it remains unknown how these distinct history-dependent effects – of internal and external origin – compare, and how they vary across individuals and modalities. Here, we compared the effects of serial dependence with or without trial-by-trial feedback on perceptual decisions regarding vestibular or visual self-motion stimuli, and examined how they relate to autistic traits. Seventy healthy adults performed a two-alternative forced-choice heading discrimination task with visual or vestibular cues (separately), under two conditions: with feedback indicating whether each choice was correct or incorrect, and without feedback. Without feedback, significant choice repetition was seen in both modalities. Surprisingly, on average correct-choice feedback did not enhance the choice repetition effect; rather it remained similar in magnitude. By contrast, incorrect-choice feedback led to a large, opposite effect – choice switching – which was stronger for vestibular cues. Thus, only error signals, not confirmatory signals, modulated serial dependence. Choice repetition in the no-feedback vestibular condition correlated significantly with autistic traits. This relationship was not observed when feedback was provided. The similarity in choice repetition following correct-choice feedback vs. no feedback was more pronounced in individuals with high autism traits, and suggests that positive serial dependence (without feedback) may reflect an internal assumption that one’s decisions were mostly correct.

title: Spatiotemporal cognitive modes underlying theory of mind: A Human Connectome Project fMRI study
description: Theory of mind (ToM), or mentalization, is a core aspect of social cognition that enables individuals to infer the mental states of others. Brain activity underlying ToM has been extensively studied using functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI), with a primary focus on the Default Mode (DM). We investigated the interplay of multiple cognitive modes (i.e., cognitive processes eliciting distinct fMRI-derived spatiotemporal patterns) engaged during ToM, extending beyond but still including the DM. We analyzed a large-scale fMRI dataset from the Human Connectome Project Social (HCP) Cognition task, where participants (n=500) evaluated whether movements of animated shapes depicted social or random interactions. Six cognitive modes were retrieved using constrained principal component analysis (CPCA): (1) Multiple Demand (MD), (2) Default Mode B (DM-B), (3) Auditory Attention for Response (AAR), (4) Focus on Visual Features (FVF), (5) Language (LAN), and (6) Re-Evaluation (RE-EV). The functions of these cognitive modes have been characterized based on analyses of their task-induced blood-oxygen-level-dependent (BOLD) changes over many tasks, but in the context of the HCP Social Cognition task, their BOLD changes were interpreted as: (1) mentally projecting into social narratives (DM-B), (2) allocating external attention to task-relevant components (MD), (3) processing non-verbal communication (LAN), (4) focusing on visual features of the social stimuli (FVF), (5) suppressing auditory attention due to increased visual attention (AAR), and (6) re-evaluating for possibly missed social patterns (RE-EV). Thus, our results confirmed the activation of DM during ToM, but also specified the contributions of five additional cognitive modes. An application of this work is that the six sets of mode-aligned functional patterns can serve as separate inputs for machine learning algorithms aimed at subtyping patients with psychiatric conditions to advance toward precision psychiatry.
